content:
# Leviticus Chapter 22 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 71r–71v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_022_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_022_page_002.jpg` | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 108–109

---

**1** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**2** "Speak to Aaron and his sons, that they separate themselves from the holy things of the sons of Israel, and that they not profane my holy name in the things that they consecrate to me: I am the LORD.

**3** Say to them: 'Throughout your generations, if any man of all your offspring approaches the holy things that the sons of Israel consecrate to the LORD while he has uncleanness upon him, that person shall be cut off from before me: I am the LORD.

**4** No man of the offspring of Aaron who is a leper or who has a discharge shall eat of the holy things until he is clean. Whoever touches anything unclean from a corpse, or a man from whom there is an emission of semen,

**5** or a man who touches any swarming thing by which he would be made unclean, or any person by whom he would be made unclean — whatever his uncleanness —

**6** the person who touches any such thing shall be unclean until evening and shall not eat of the holy things unless he has bathed his body in water.

**7** When the sun goes down he shall be clean, and afterward he may eat of the holy things, for they are his food.

**8** He shall not eat what dies of itself or what is torn by beasts, becoming unclean by it: I am the LORD.

**9** They shall therefore keep my charge, lest they bear sin for it and die thereby when they profane it: I am the LORD who sanctifies them.

**10** No outsider shall eat of the holy things; no sojourner of the priest's household nor hired servant shall eat of the holy things.

**11** But if a priest buys a slave as his property with money, the slave may eat of it, and those who are born in his house may eat of his food.

**12** If a priest's daughter marries an outsider, she shall not eat of the contribution of the holy things.

**13** But if a priest's daughter is widowed or divorced and has no children and returns to her father's house, as in her youth, she may eat of her father's food. Yet no outsider shall eat of it.

**14** And if a man eats of the holy things inadvertently, he shall add a fifth of its value to it and give the holy thing to the priest.

**15** They shall not profane the holy things of the sons of Israel, which they contribute to the LORD,

**16** and so cause them to bear iniquity and guilt, by eating their holy things: for I am the LORD who sanctifies them.'"

**17** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**18** "Speak to Aaron and his sons and all the sons of Israel and say to them: When any man of the house of Israel or of the sojourners in Israel presents his offering, whether in fulfillment of a vow or as a freewill offering, which they offer to the LORD as a burnt offering —

**19** to be accepted you shall offer a male without blemish, from the cattle, from the sheep, or from the goats.

**20** You shall not offer anything that has a blemish, for it will not be accepted for you.

**21** And when a man offers a sacrifice of peace offerings to the LORD to fulfill a vow or as a freewill offering, from the herd or from the flock, it must be without blemish to be accepted; there shall be no blemish in it.

**22** Animals blind, or broken, or maimed, or having a discharge, or an itch, or scabs — you shall not offer these to the LORD or give them as a food offering on the altar to the LORD.

**23** A bull or a lamb that has a limb too long or too short — that you may offer as a freewill offering, but for a vow it shall not be accepted.

**24** Any animal that has its testicles bruised or crushed or torn or cut you shall not offer to the LORD; you shall not do this within your land.

**25** Neither shall you offer as the bread of your God any such animals obtained from a foreigner. Since there is a blemish in them, because of their deformity, they will not be accepted for you.'"

**26** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**27** "When a bull or a sheep or a goat is born, it shall remain seven days with its mother, and from the eighth day on it shall be acceptable as a food offering to the LORD.

**28** But you shall not kill an ox or a sheep and her young both in one day.

**29** And when you offer a sacrifice of thanksgiving to the LORD, you shall offer it so that you may be accepted.

**30** It shall be eaten on the same day; you shall leave none of it until morning: I am the LORD.

**31** So you shall keep my commandments and do them: I am the LORD.

**32** And you shall not profane my holy name, that I may be sanctified among the sons of Israel. I am the LORD who sanctifies you,

**33** who brought you out of the land of Egypt to be your God: I am the LORD."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations

**Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 71r–71v:**
Leviticus 22 occupies folios 71r and 71v in the Leningrad Codex, following immediately upon chapter 21's legislation concerning priestly blemishes and marriage restrictions. The chapter divides cleanly into three literary blocks: (1) vv. 1–16, governing priestly access to the holy things — which priests may eat them, under what conditions, and who from the priest's household may share in them; (2) vv. 17–25, establishing the requirement that sacrificial animals be without blemish (tamim), parallel to the priestly blemish-legislation of ch. 21; and (3) vv. 26–33, a miscellany of additional ordinances concerning the age of animals, the prohibition on slaughtering a mother and offspring on the same day, the thank offering, and the grand theological summary in vv. 31–33 invoking the prohibition on profaning the holy name and the Exodus formula.

The chapter's literary structure is governed by the repeated divine speech-formulas: the LORD spoke to Moses (vv. 1, 17, 26) introduce each of the three major sections. Within each section, the self-identification formula "I am the LORD" (ani YHWH) appears at structurally significant points — the closing of specific prohibitions or warrant-clauses — occurring in vv. 2, 3, 8, 9, 16, 30, 31, 32, 33. This pervasive repetition of the divine name-formula is the highest concentration of the formula in any Leviticus chapter, underscoring that everything in ch. 22 flows from YHWH's sovereign identity as the one who sanctifies Israel.

The Masoretic text in B19A is well-preserved throughout. The chapter contains one of the most important kiddush ha-Shem passages in the Torah: v. 32's "you shall not profane my holy name, that I may be sanctified among the sons of Israel" — the theological nerve-center of the entire Holiness Code (chs. 17–26), and arguably of the Priestly theological program as a whole.

**Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 108–109:**
Leviticus 22 in the Vaticanus spans pages 108 and 109 (imaged as `LEV_022_vaticanus_page_001.jpg` and `LEV_022_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`). The LXX of this chapter is generally a faithful rendering of the MT, though several lexical and structural divergences appear — particularly in the blemish-catalogue of vv. 21–25 and in the rendering of key technical terms such as tamim, zar, and the nazar-root in v. 2. The LXX translators faced significant challenges in rendering the priestly vocabulary of vv. 3–9, where multiple technical terms for impurity-categories appear in rapid sequence.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. "They shall separate themselves from the holy things" (v. 2) — ve-yinnazeru mi-qodshei bney-Yisra'el:**
The verb nazar (Niphal, "to separate oneself, to abstain, to consecrate oneself by withdrawal") in v. 2 is the same root as the Nazirite (nazir — one who separates himself by vow). Here it governs the priests' required abstention from the holy food when they are in a state of ritual uncleanness. The verse is the thematic statement of the chapter's first section: priests must "separate themselves" — deliberately withdraw from contact with the holy things — whenever they are ritually unfit. The rendering "that they separate themselves from the holy things" (following ESV/NASB) preserves the nazar-root's connotation of active, deliberate separation rather than mere omission.

The phrase "the holy things of the sons of Israel" (qodshei bney Yisra'el) encompasses all the sacred food portions that the Israelites bring as offerings and that devolve to the priests as their portion: the most holy things (grain offerings, sin offerings, guilt offerings) and the holy things (peace-offering portions, firstfruits, tithes in kind). The priests are stewards of Israel's sacred donations, and that stewardship requires ritual qualification.

**2. "Profane my holy name" (v. 2) — ve-lo yechalelu et-shem qodshi:**
The combination of chalal (Piel, "to profane, desecrate") + shem qodshi ("my holy name") is the defining prohibition of vv. 2 and 32, and one of the most theologically consequential expressions in the Torah. The concept of chillul ha-Shem (profanation of the Name) and its obverse, kiddush ha-Shem (sanctification of the Name, v. 32), constitute the Holiness Code's central theological axis: all priestly conduct either sanctifies or profanes YHWH's name in the eyes of Israel and the nations. An unclean priest eating the holy things profanes not merely himself but the divine name itself, because the holy things are YHWH's and eating them in an unfit state treats YHWH's holiness as negligible.

The rendering "profane my holy name" is the standard ESV/NASB/KJV rendering. Alternatives such as "dishonor" or "desecrate" are weaker and miss the specific sense of chalal (to make common what is holy, to pierce through the boundary between sacred and profane).

**3. "Shall be cut off from before me" (v. 3) — ve-nikhretah ha-nefesh ha-hi mi-lefanay:**
The karet ("cutting off") penalty is the Holiness Code's standard sanction for deliberate violations of the most serious prohibitions. The formula ve-nikhretah ha-nefesh ha-hi ("that person shall be cut off") occurs throughout Leviticus and Numbers for offenses that defile the sanctuary or treat YHWH's sacred things with contempt. The precise nature of karet is debated: it may denote (a) early death by divine agency, (b) exclusion from the covenant community, (c) exclusion from the afterlife or from the assembly of one's ancestors, or (d) some combination of the above. The phrase here uniquely specifies mi-lefanay ("from before me") — cut off from YHWH's presence — which emphasizes the relational dimension of the penalty: the violator loses standing before YHWH himself.

The rendering "that person shall be cut off from before me" (NASB/ESV: "shall be cut off from my presence") preserves the literal spatial-relational formula. "Excommunicated" (some modern versions) is too narrow; "die" (some paraphrases) is too specific.

**4. "Leper" (v. 4) — tsara'at:**
The term tsara'at (מְצֹרָע — the Pual participle of tsara', the one "stricken with tsara'at") designates the skin condition treated at length in Leviticus 13–14. Modern scholars debate whether tsara'at corresponds to Hansen's disease (leprosy) in the modern medical sense or encompasses a broader range of skin conditions. The traditional English rendering "leprosy" / "leper" (KJV, ESV, NASB) reflects the longstanding translation tradition, but it risks anachronism. LOGOS renders "leper" as the formal equivalent, consistent with the ESV/NASB tradition and with the rendering used in LEV_013–014.

**5. "Discharge" (v. 4) — zav:**
The condition of zov (זוֹב) — an abnormal bodily discharge, treated in Leviticus 15 — renders a priest unfit to eat the holy things. The term encompass various types of genital discharge (male: ch. 15:1–15; female: ch. 15:19–30). The rendering "discharge" (ESV, NASB) is standard.

**6. "Unclean from a corpse" (v. 4) — tamei le-nefesh:**
The phrase tamei le-nefesh ("unclean from/for a corpse") uses nefesh in the same technical priestly sense as in 21:1 (nefesh as corpse). Corpse-uncleanness is the most severe grade of ritual impurity in the priestly system (requiring a seven-day purification using the red heifer ashes, Num 19), and it is specifically applied here to a priest who would otherwise have access to the holy food.

**7. "Emission of semen" (v. 4) — asher tetse mimmennu shikhovah-zara':**
The phrase asher tetse mimmenhu shikhovat-zara' ("from whom goes out an emission of semen") is the priestly technical term for a male seminal emission (whether from intercourse or nocturnal emission), treated in Lev 15:16–18. Such a man is unclean until evening after bathing. The rendering "an emission of semen" (ESV) is formal and precise; "seminal emission" (NASB) is equally acceptable. The formal equivalent is preferred over euphemisms.

**8. "Outsider" (v. 10) — zar:**
The term zar (זָר, "outsider, stranger, foreigner") in the priestly legislation of Leviticus 22 does not primarily denote ethnic foreignness but rather priestly-status exclusion. A zar is anyone who is not a member of the Aaronic priestly family — whether Israelite layperson or ethnic foreigner. The contrast in vv. 10–13 makes this clear: the zar (outsider) is distinguished from (a) the priest's own purchased slave, (b) those born in his household, and (c) his widowed or divorced daughter who has returned to his household — all of whom may eat the holy food. The sole criterion is membership within the priestly household, not ethnicity.

The rendering "outsider" (following ESV) is preferred over "stranger" (KJV) or "layman" (NASB), since "layman" introduces a later ecclesiastical concept, and "stranger" in modern English connotes unfamiliarity rather than legal-status exclusion. "Outsider" captures the priestly-insider/outsider binary precisely.

**9. "Sojourner" / "hired servant" (v. 10) — toshav / sakir:**
The toshav ("sojourner, resident alien") and sakir ("hired servant") form a pair that defines the outer limits of the priest's non-priestly household. The toshav is a long-term resident alien living within the household who does not belong to the priest's family or slave household; the sakir is a day-laborer or hired employee with a contractual but temporary relationship. Neither qualifies for the holy food, because neither is an organic member of the priestly household. The contrast with the priest's purchased slave (v. 11 — who may eat) is economically striking: the slave has a permanent, ownership-based belonging to the household that the hired man lacks.

The rendering "sojourner" and "hired servant" (ESV) preserves the legal-status distinction. "Tenant" (some versions for toshav) is too specific; "sojourner" captures the resident-but-not-belonging status.

**10. "Buys a slave as his property with money" (v. 11) — qinyan kaspo:**
The phrase qinyan kaspo ("purchase/acquisition of his silver") denotes a slave acquired by monetary purchase — chattel slavery in the ancient Near Eastern sense. The rendered phrase "buys a slave as his property with money" follows the literal MT and ESV/NASB tradition. The slave so purchased (and those born in the household — yalid bayit, "born of the house") become integral members of the priestly household and therefore share in the priestly food. This is an application of the household-membership principle: the slave's belonging to the priest's household is organic (by purchase or birth) rather than contractual (like the hired man), and thus qualifies the slave for the priestly portions.

**11. "Priest's daughter who marries an outsider" (v. 12) — u-vat-kohen ki tihyeh le-ish zar:**
The case of the priest's daughter who "marries an outsider" (literally: "belongs to a man who is a zar," i.e., a non-priest) is the obverse of v. 13. When she marries out of the priestly household, she enters her husband's household and loses her priestly-household status. She therefore may not eat of the priestly contributions. But if she is widowed or divorced and returns childless to her father's house (v. 13), she returns to priestly-household status and may eat again "as in her youth." The phrase "as in her youth" (ki-ne'ureha) is a poignant expression: she returns to the status she had before her marriage, as if the intervening years had not removed her from her father's house.

The condition of returning childless (v'zera ayn lah — "and she has no offspring") is significant: if she has children, they belong to her deceased or divorced husband's household lineage and would constitute a link to that other household, complicating her return to purely priestly-household status.

**12. "Eats inadvertently" (v. 14) — be-shgagah:**
The adverb bi-shgagah ("inadvertently, by mistake, in error") specifies that the restitution law of v. 14 applies to unintentional eating of the holy things — the case where a layperson unknowingly consumed priestly food. The penalty is: return the value of the holy food + add one-fifth (a 20% surcharge). This is the same restitution formula applied to other inadvertent violations of sacred property (Lev 5:16). Deliberate eating of the holy things as a layperson would presumably fall under the karet penalty of v. 3.

The rendering "inadvertently" (ESV, NASB) captures the bi-shgagah concept precisely. "By mistake" is acceptable; "accidentally" (some versions) is slightly too colloquial.

**13. "Cause them to bear iniquity and guilt" (v. 16) — ve-his'u otam avon ashmah:**
Verse 16 supplies the theological rationale for the priestly-food access regulations: failure to enforce these boundaries would "cause them [the sons of Israel] to bear iniquity and guilt" (avon ashmah). The phrase is double-weighted: avon (guilt, iniquity, the culpable act itself) and ashmah (guilt, the state of being liable for an offense). The rendering "cause them to bear iniquity and guilt" preserves both terms. The subject of "them" is disputed — it may refer to (a) the sons of Israel who inadvertently eat holy food and incur guilt, or (b) the priests who fail to guard the holy things and thereby cause profanation. The rendering is kept structurally ambiguous in line with the MT.

**14. "Without blemish" (vv. 19, 20, 21, 25) — tamim:**
The term tamim (תָּמִים, "whole, complete, without defect/blemish") is the key term of the sacrificial-animal legislation in vv. 17–25, appearing as the positive requirement (v. 21: tamim yihyeh — "it must be without blemish") and its negation (v. 20: kol asher-bo mum lo takrivu — "you shall not offer anything that has a blemish"). Tamim is the animal-sacrifice correlate of the priestly body-integrity requirement (mum, "blemish") in ch. 21: both the priest and the animal offered must meet the same standard of completeness before YHWH.

The formal equivalent "without blemish" (ESV, NASB, KJV) is the standard rendering. "Without defect" (NIV) is acceptable but loses the specific blemish-vocabulary that links the animal requirements to the priestly-body requirements of ch. 21 where mum is used.

**15. "A limb too long or too short" (v. 23) — sarua' o qaluah:**
Verse 23 introduces a nuanced case: an animal with sarua' ("extended, elongated — a limb too long") or qaluah ("contracted, shortened — a limb too short"). This pair denotes proportional asymmetry — one limb disproportionately longer or shorter than its counterpart. Such an animal may be used for a freewill offering (nedavah) but not for a vow-offering (neder). The distinction is significant: a freewill offering is spontaneous and voluntary (lower grade), while a vow-offering is obligatory (the offerer bound himself by vow to bring it). Higher obligation requires higher quality. The rendering "a limb too long or too short" (ESV, NASB: "one limb overgrown or stunted") preserves the paired contrast.

**16. "Bruised or crushed or torn or cut" (v. 24) — mahuts o kathut o nathuq o karot:**
Verse 24 lists four conditions of testicular damage (all four terms apply to the 'ashekhim — "the testicles") that disqualify an animal from sacrifice: (a) mahuts ("bruised, damaged by blows"), (b) kathut ("crushed, ground — more severe damage"), (c) nathuq ("torn, wrenched apart"), (d) karot ("cut off — castrated"). These four terms constitute a spectrum of male-genital injury from bruising to castration. Animals castrated (karot) are entirely disqualified; those with lesser damage are similarly barred. The prohibition "you shall not do this within your land" may refer to the practice of castrating animals generally, or specifically to the practice of castrating sacrificial animals — the former reading is followed by NASB and most commentators.

The rendering "bruised or crushed or torn or cut" follows the four-term literal sequence. ESV: "castrated by bruising or crushing or tearing or cutting" — the ESV's insertion of "castrated" as the governing verb interprets all four terms as forms of emasculation, which is defensible but goes beyond the MT's plain listing.

**17. "Animals obtained from a foreigner" (v. 25) — mi-yad ben-nekhar:**
The phrase "from the hand of a foreigner" (mi-yad ben-nekhar — "from the hand of a son of a foreigner") specifies animals acquired from a non-Israelite. Such animals, even if apparently unblemished, are disqualified "because there is a blemish in them, because of their deformity" — the text implies either (a) that animals from foreigners are presumed to have hidden blemishes (since the Israelite regulations for animal care and selection were not observed), or (b) a general disqualification of foreign-acquired animals from altar use. The phrase "because of their deformity" (ki moshchatam bahem — "for their corruption is in them") suggests the former: a presumed defect in the animal's essential character or condition.

**18. "Remain seven days with its mother" (v. 27) — shiv'at yamim yihyeh im-immo:**
The legislation of vv. 27–28 introduces a pair of related "compassion for animals" ordinances. First (v. 27): a newborn animal must remain with its mother for seven days before becoming eligible for sacrifice. The seven-day period parallels the eight-day circumcision requirement (the eighth day is when the newborn enters the covenant; the eighth day is similarly when the animal enters sacrificial eligibility — vv. 27–28). The animal cannot be brought to the altar before its eighth day. Second (v. 28): a mother animal and its young must not be slaughtered on the same day. This prohibition has no stated rationale in the text but is widely understood as a compassion ordinance — a prohibition on a brutality that would be excessive even within the system of animal sacrifice.

**19. "And you shall not kill an ox or a sheep and her young both in one day" (v. 28) — ve-shor o-seh oto ve-et-beno lo tishchatu be-yom echad:**
The prohibition oto ve-et-beno ("it and its young") applies gender-inclusively (MT uses a female suffix -beno for the young, applied to a male ox or female sheep alike). The law targets the specific scenario of mother-offspring sacrifice on the same day, not any two related animals. Maimonides (Guide 3:48) attributes this law to compassion: "the pain of the animals at the loss of their offspring is very great." Whether this is correct is debated, but the law's structural parallel to the bird-nest law of Deut 22:6–7 (also an offspring-protection ordinance) suggests a similar motivation.

**20. "So that you may be accepted" (v. 29) — lirtzonkhem tizbachu:**
The phrase lirtzonkhem (literally "for your acceptance/pleasure") governs the thank-offering (todah) legislation: it must be offered "so that you may be accepted." The word ratson (רָצוֹן, "pleasure, acceptance, favor") is the key liturgical term for a sacrifice's successful reception — when YHWH "accepts" (ratsah) the offering, it achieves its purpose. The rendering "so that you may be accepted" (ESV, NASB) preserves the technical liturgical sense. The todah (thank offering) must be consumed on the day of its offering (v. 30), unlike other peace offerings (which may be eaten over two days, Lev 7:15–17). This same-day rule ensures the celebratory character of the todah — it is an immediate, fresh celebration of a specific divine act of deliverance.

**21. "You shall not profane my holy name" (v. 32) — ve-lo techalelu et-shem qodshi:**
This verse contains the theological summit of Leviticus 22 and arguably of the entire Holiness Code. Three elements compose the verse: (a) the negative prohibition — "you shall not profane my holy name" (lo techalelu et-shem qodshi); (b) the positive corollary — "that I may be sanctified among the sons of Israel" (ve-niqdashti be-tokh bney Yisra'el); (c) the divine self-identification formula — "I am the LORD who sanctifies you" (ani YHWH meqadeshkhem).

The pairing of chillul ha-Shem (profanation of the Name) with kiddush ha-Shem (sanctification of the Name) creates a theo-ethical binary that structures all of priestly ethics: every action either profanes or sanctifies YHWH's name among Israel and the nations. The later rabbinic concept of kiddush ha-Shem — martyrdom, righteous conduct, anything that causes the divine name to be revered — has its roots in this verse. The rendering "you shall not profane my holy name, that I may be sanctified among the sons of Israel" follows the MT syntax precisely and captures the paired negative-positive structure.

**22. "Who brought you out of the land of Egypt to be your God" (v. 33) — ha-motsi etkhem me-erets Mitsraim lihyot lakhem le-Elohim:**
The closing verse appends the Exodus-formula to the sanctification formula, as frequently at the close of major Leviticus sections (cf. 11:45; 19:36; 25:38, 42, 55; 26:13). The formula identifies YHWH as the God who acted historically to deliver Israel from Egypt and who therefore stands as their God by right of redemption. The priestly regulations of ch. 22 — complex as they are — are grounded not in abstract holiness-logic alone but in the historical reality of the Exodus: YHWH is Israel's God because he brought them out; therefore Israel must live according to his standards of holiness. The rendering follows the MT directly.

---

### LXX Vaticanus Variants

| Verse | MT (Hebrew) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Note |
|-------|-------------|-----------------|------|
| 2 | ve-yinnazeru mi-qodshei bney-Yisra'el ("they shall separate themselves from the holy things of the sons of Israel") | ἀποχωρισθήτωσαν ἀπὸ τῶν ἁγίων υἱῶν Ισραηλ ("they shall be separated from the holy things of the sons of Israel") | LXX: ἀποχωρίζω ("to separate, withdraw") for nazar (Niphal); the LXX renders the passive/reflexive form and preserves the basic sense |
| 2 | ve-lo yechalelu et-shem qodshi ("and not profane my holy name") | καὶ οὐ βεβηλώσουσιν τὸ ὄνομα τὸ ἅγιόν μου ("and they shall not profane my holy name") | LXX: βεβηλόω ("to profane") for chalal (Piel); precise equivalent; this is the standard LXX rendering throughout |
| 3 | ve-nikhretah ha-nefesh ha-hi mi-lefanay ("that person shall be cut off from before me") | ἐξολεθρευθήσεται ἡ ψυχὴ ἐκείνη ἀπ' ἐμοῦ ("that soul shall be utterly destroyed from me") | LXX: ἐξολεθρεύω ("to utterly destroy, exterminate") for karet; the LXX intensifies the penalty with the "utterly destroy" language, reflecting its interpretation of karet as complete destruction |
| 4 | metsora' ("leper") | λεπρῶν ("leper") | LXX: λεπρός — direct equivalent; the LXX consistently uses this term for tsara'at |
| 4 | tamei le-nefesh ("unclean from a corpse") | ἀκάθαρτος ἔσται ἐπὶ ψυχῇ ("unclean upon a soul/corpse") | LXX: ψυχή for nefesh in the corpse-sense; consistent with LXX treatment elsewhere (cf. ch. 21) |
| 10 | zar ("outsider") | ἀλλογενής ("one of another race/family") | LXX: ἀλλογενής (literally "of another birth/family") rather than ξένος ("stranger/foreigner"); ἀλλογενής better captures the priestly-status sense of zar (not ethnic foreigner but one not of the priestly family) |
| 10 | toshav kohen ve-sakir ("sojourner of the priest's household nor hired servant") | πάροικος ἱερέως ἢ μισθωτός ("sojourner of a priest or hired man") | LXX: πάροικος for toshav (standard LXX rendering); μισθωτός for sakir (standard; also used in John 10:12–13 for the hireling) |
| 11 | qinyan kaspo ("property of his money/purchase") | κτῆμα ἀργυρίου αὐτοῦ ("a property/possession acquired with his silver") | LXX: κτῆμα for qinyan; both denote "acquired property/possession"; the LXX preserves the monetary-acquisition sense |
| 14 | bi-shgagah ("inadvertently") | ἀγνοίᾳ ("in ignorance") | LXX: ἄγνοια for shgagah; a reasonable rendering — both terms indicate unintentional error; ἄγνοια emphasizes cognitive unawareness; shgagah emphasizes inadvertent stumbling |
| 19 | tamim ("without blemish") | ἄμωμον ("without blemish") | LXX: ἄμωμος is the standard LXX rendering for tamim in sacrificial contexts; this is the same word used in the NT for Christ as the "unblemished" sacrifice (1 Pet 1:19) |
| 20 | kol asher-bo mum ("anything that has a blemish in it") | πᾶν ὃ ἂν ἔχῃ μῶμον ἐν αὐτῷ ("everything that has a blemish in it") | LXX: μῶμος for mum (direct loanword or lexical equivalent); identical structural correspondence |
| 22 | ivveret o shvurah o chaluzah o yabbelet o garav o yalefet ("blind or broken or maimed or having a discharge or an itch or scabs") | τυφλὸν ἢ συντετριμμένον ἢ κολόβωμα ἔχον ἢ καλλωπίζεσθαι μὴ δυνάμενον ἢ ψωριῶν ἢ λειχῆνι ("blind or crushed or having a mutilation or unable to be made fair or having mange or a scaly skin disease") | LXX: significant divergences in the middle terms — chaluzah (MT: "maimed, having a slit") becomes κολόβωμα ("mutilation"); yabbelet (MT: "discharge/excrescence") is rendered with a phrase about being unable to be made beautiful; a substantially different blemish catalogue |
| 23 | sarua' o qaluah ("a limb too long or too short") | ὑπερσύντομον ἢ κολοβόν ("overly contracted or mutilated/short") | LXX: ὑπερσύντομος for sarua' (an unusual choice — the LXX appears to read sarua' as "overly compressed" rather than "elongated"); κολοβόν for qaluah (better attested) |
| 24 | mahuts o kathut o nathuq o karot ("bruised or crushed or torn or cut") | θλαδίας ἢ ἀνορχίας ἢ ἐκτομίας ἢ ἐξαιρέσει ("bruised-testicled or without testicles or surgically castrated or by removal") | LXX: a remarkable four-term rendering that matches each MT term with a specific medical vocabulary: θλαδίας (bruised genitals), ἀνορχίας (without testicles), ἐκτομίας (cut/castrated), ἐξαιρέσει (by removal) — more explicit than the MT |
| 27 | shiv'at yamim yihyeh im-immo ("it shall remain seven days with its mother") | ἑπτὰ ἡμέρας ἔσται ὑπὸ τὴν μητέρα ("seven days it shall be under its mother") | LXX: ὑπό ("under") for im ("with"); a slight reframing: "under" its mother (nursing position/dependency) rather than simply "with" its mother |
| 28 | oto ve-et-beno lo tishchatu be-yom echad ("it and its young you shall not slaughter on the same day") | καὶ ταῦρον ἢ πρόβατον αὐτὸ καὶ τὰ παιδία αὐτοῦ οὐ σφάξεις ἐν μιᾷ ἡμέρᾳ ("neither a bull nor a sheep — it and its young — you shall slaughter on one day") | LXX: adds an explanatory specification (bull or sheep) not in the MT at this point; παιδία ("children/young") for -beno; essentially equivalent |
| 29 | lirtzonkhem tizbachu ("you shall offer it for your acceptance") | πρὸς δεκτὸν ὑμῶν θύσετε ("for your acceptance you shall sacrifice") | LXX: δεκτός ("acceptable, received") for ratson; functionally equivalent |
| 32 | ve-niqdashti be-tokh bney Yisra'el ("that I may be sanctified among the sons of Israel") | καὶ ἁγιασθήσομαι ἐν τοῖς υἱοῖς Ισραηλ ("and I shall be sanctified among the sons of Israel") | LXX: ἁγιάζω for qadash; precise equivalent; the passive "I shall be sanctified" captures the Niphal ve-niqdashti exactly |
| 33 | ha-motsi etkhem me-erets Mitsraim lihyot lakhem le-Elohim ("who brought you out of the land of Egypt to be your God") | ὁ ἐξαγαγὼν ὑμᾶς ἐκ γῆς Αἰγύπτου εἶναι ὑμῶν θεός ("who brought you out of the land of Egypt to be your God") | LXX: precise structural equivalent; ἐξάγω for yatsa' Hiphil (standard Exodus-formula rendering throughout the LXX) |

---

### Consistent Term Renderings

| Hebrew Term | LOGOS Rendering | Notes |
|-------------|-----------------|-------|
| YHWH | the LORD | Small capitals; standard throughout |
| bney Yisra'el | sons of Israel | vv. 2, 3, 15, 18, 32; the Israelite congregation |
| qodshei bney Yisra'el | the holy things of the sons of Israel | v. 2; the full range of priestly food portions |
| nazar (Niphal) | separate themselves | v. 2; deliberate abstention from the holy things while unclean |
| shem qodshi | my holy name | vv. 2, 32; the divine Name as the locus of holiness |
| chalal (Piel) | profane | vv. 2, 9, 15, 32; desecrate; the chapter's key negative verb |
| karet | cut off | v. 3; the divine penalty for deliberate profanation |
| tsara'at / metsora' | leper | v. 4; the skin condition treated in Lev 13–14 |
| zav | discharge | v. 4; abnormal bodily flux |
| zar | outsider | vv. 10, 12, 13; one not of the priestly household |
| toshav | sojourner | v. 10; resident alien of the priestly household |
| sakir | hired servant | v. 10; contractual day-laborer |
| qinyan kaspo | slave as his property with money | v. 11; chattel purchased by monetary acquisition |
| bi-shgagah | inadvertently | v. 14; unintentional error |
| tamim | without blemish | vv. 19, 20, 21, 25; complete, whole; the sacrificial-animal standard |
| mum | blemish | vv. 20, 21, 25; physical defect disqualifying from sacrifice |
| sarua' | a limb too long | v. 23; elongated limb; the freewill-offering exception |
| qaluah | too short | v. 23; contracted/shortened limb |
| mahuts / kathut / nathuq / karot | bruised / crushed / torn / cut | v. 24; four degrees of testicular damage |
| nedavah | freewill offering | v. 23; spontaneous voluntary offering (lower standard) |
| neder | vow | vv. 18, 21, 23; obligatory offering by prior commitment (higher standard) |
| todah | thanksgiving / thank offering | v. 29; the peace-offering sub-type offered in gratitude |
| ratson / lirtzonkhem | accepted / so that you may be accepted | v. 29; the liturgical reception-formula |
| kiddush ha-Shem | [sanctification of the Name — see v. 32 note] | v. 32; the positive corollary of the chapter's central prohibition |
| meqadeshkhem | who sanctifies you | vv. 32; YHWH as the active agent of Israel's holiness |
| ha-motsi etkhem me-erets Mitsraim | who brought you out of the land of Egypt | v. 33; the Exodus formula grounding all priestly legislation |

---

— LOGOS
